name: Emory Dewitt Alexander, B.S.A.
description: Alexander the Great II, like the illustrious man whose name he bears, is the great military genius of the university war department. For four years he has sacrificed his time and energy for the good of this department, and no butcher ever achieved more grace in wielding the sword than he. It is said that his spirit is dominated by a shielding shadow which is ever whispering in his ear to bone, bone, bone, and during the hours between taps and reveille he faithfully obeys this command by plunging into the depths contained in the pages of botany, chemistry, or "The Extermination of the Boll-weevil in Alaska." His efforts have not been in vain and on that ladder of class-room distinction few rounds remain to be climbed by him. His faithfulness, however, has destroyed his gift for politicking and he has utterly failed to extract a proctorship or any other form of graft, or this fact is due to his being unable to assume those owl-like looks which count so much in this profession.

name: Spurgeon Earl Alverson, B.S.E
description: Spurgeon is a quiet, soft-spoken kind of a fellow, who gets his county paper from the city of Sugar Valley. (For his location consult your annotated map.) He arrived at the university with firm intentions of becoming a scientific plow-boy, but growing weary of climbing the "Ag." hill five times per day he shifted his anchorage to the buildings this side of the branch. Since that time his efforts have been to make of himself a scientific pedagogist instead of a plow-gogist. He is afflicted with a craving for math., and delights in letting his mind penetrate into the infinite depths of those figures which convey no meaning to the average human. To many who do not know him, Earl is more or less a mystery; every act, every move, is concealed by an unassuming shield of modesty. Although you find him reserved, yet go deeper and there you will find the true metal.

name: J. GORDON ASHLEY
description: Nobody can inveigle him into telling just what the "J" stands for. We fear, however, that it stands for Jackass. Gordon roams the streets with "Tad" Paine betimes, the only difference between the two runts being that Tad is president of the Y. M. C. A., but J. Gordon not even a member. He is a most ambitious cuss, and held up the G. O. P. party twice in succession to let him tryout for manager. Now he is Lord High Team Manager and Honorable Chief Flunky for the baseball team. This man is so stingy that he makes the Georgia team play with one ball all season. Nevertheless, they say that he is going to allow a new ball for the Tech series. Gordon used to start all the neighborhood hounds to howling as a leading member of the old "Barefoot Quartet." Valdosta is his home hamlet.

name: CARTER SHEPHARD BALDWIN, JR.
clubs: chief justice of the Honor Tribunal
description: "Abe" is the best example we have of one who has risen in spite of difficulties. Chief among these being his various and sundry roommates. He has enjoyed great popularity among the members of his class, who have expressed their confidence and appreciation by bestowing upon him the offices of chaplain, poet, chief justice of the Honor Tribunal, president of Demosthenian, etc. He has risen high in Y. M. C. A. circles, being one of their most faithful cohorts at their weekly hot-dog banquets. In looking for new worlds to conquer, his attention was attracted to the military department, and he now wields the sabre with all the ease and grace of one accustomed to swinging an axe in the primeval forests (of Morgan). He has been a good student, however, and as the years go by, we expect to hear from him as a successful advocate of the "lickin' an' learnin'" theory in some old district school.

name: Robert Daniel Bedinger
description: As the Atlanta yellow sheets would begin, "Here is another Atlanta boy." Bob once saw a mule strolling about the streets of that horn-tooting city, so he decided to come to Georgia and take agriculture. At the very outset Bob grew discouraged. Finally, getting a certain felicitous bovine seated upon the three-legged milking stool in dairy "lab." one day, he asked the instructor which bulb he should squeeze to get buttermilk. He has learned better by now, and no longer buries eggs in order to grow eggplants. Bob does everything by schedule, and on one occasion was disconcerted for the whole day on account of being two minutes late for breakfast. Georgia loves a regular man!

name: Eugene Robert Black, Jr.
description: When Gene first arrived at the university we thought he was the long-leggedest boy we had ever seen, but he has kept right on growing ever since. During his early college years his favorite occupation was shooting Bobby Mac and Jake Lustrat, and trying to get out of drill with an occasional flyer in politics. Since then he has broadened out considerably, being quite a manipulator of a billiard cue or mid-iron, having no superior as a "manicurist." It is assumed that he and McClellan room together so as to get more chance for practice in the latter gentle art. If E. R. Jr. can make Phi Beta Kappa with such a minimum expenditure of energy as he used then there's no telling what he will do when he starts in on a regulation eight-hour day and goes to bed at night.

name: Alonzo Plumb Boardman
description: Lonnie came to us four years ago from Augusta, a green, sporty kind of a freshman. He remained in the thick of the fight for three years and after becoming tired either of the struggle or the kind of struggle, transplanted himself up to Columbia. A funny kind of fellow he is—going off and then telegraphing back for his dip. For reasons not commonly known Lonnie was called "Squirrel" by his intimate friends. He gained this name by his alertness in hopping over rafters and hiding behind pillows on a certain occasion. But you may not yet see what gives him the above title. Suffice it to say that in one of those heart-throbbing moments when the object approached or he approached the object causing the excitement he said: "Boo! You didn't know that I was here!" Perfectly natural under the circumstances.

name: Farrar Washington Bond
description: If anybody could persuade Farrar that getting educated was as important as making a good leader of the Mandolin Club, he would lead his classes too, we guess. But up to date he has not been convinced, at least not so you could notice it. F. W. is not only a wonder on the violin, but he is equally as good when it comes to playing on a fair damsel's innocent young heart. His successes in this line are unlimited. Impassioned letters may be found in any old coat of his where he has carelessly tossed them aside, little dreaming of the havoc he has wrought. Of late he has been letting his blond locks grow wild, which, coupled with his immaculate handkerchief and never-bagged trousers, form an absolutely irresistible combination to the wariest of females. Farrar should be careful how he takes advantage of little boys. All in all Farrar is a good boy and we expect to see him a famous violinist some day. His greatest fault is allowing Bunk Reid to be too affectionate.

name: John Stovall Boyd, Jr.
description: Johnnie is a very timid little creature (about six feet) who speaks upon rare occasions only, but who honors his closest friends with startling little grunts and squeals which may be variously interpreted. Only those who know him best can understand Johnnie's love for the German language and for the society of women. His becoming modesty, however, is an asset which cannot be overlooked even though it has cost Georgia a likely football star and track man. As a critic of the screen, Johnnie is unrivaled, and Charlie Chaplin, Douglas Fairbanks, or William S. Hart can be seen to tremble with stage fright when he takes his seat upon the front row at the Strand picture show.

name: Elliot M. Braxton, Jr.
description: Ambition has been the keynote of "Brax's" career, and while in many places it has overleaped itself, yet in some cases it has not fallen short of the coveted mark. He has been an ardent militarist and his career might have been far more brilliant had not too much time been consumed in the vain effort to obtain grace in yielding the sword. "With the greatest of pleasure" he attends all chemistry labs, and absorbs very little detail of its knowledge ("good"). He is now a retired journalist, having guided the destiny of the Red and Black for a half year. "Brack," in Braxton's estimation, is about the best politician who ever turned a vote on the campus. Old chap, the class wishes you much success in the field of your endeavor.

name: William Park Brooks
description: Little Willie Brooks—from Athens, Ga., Jeffersonville Road, R. F. D.—is as quiet and unassuming as Johnnie Boyd. Nobody knows how he gets to town every morning, but if he walks he must get up at about 4:30 A. M. One morning when it snowed Willie almost got a tardy mark, but never was absent in his life. He and Carl Seagraves are living examples of the advantages of punctuality and regularity—Seagraves gets a pass, Willie got Phi Beta Kappa. 'Tis said he is going to be a preacher, and it must be so, for why does he persistently hold to his aim of devouring all the Latin and Greek in the college repertoire.

name: Walter Scott Brown
description: Brown comes from Hiawassee, "heap much Injun village," lost in the mountains of North Georgia. If you don't believe it is there, just catch the Gainesville, Northwestern and Narrow Gauge Railroad going toward Helen, Ga. Brown's savage and warlike instincts have been developed here to the extent that the commandant was afraid to refuse him a captain's commission. Besides being an irresistible ladies' man, "W. S." is also a student of social needs. He has specialized in animal husbandry for the purpose of breeding an animal whose legs on one side are shorter than those on the other. The value of such a creature lies in his ability to plow on the steep mountain sides. Brown leaves here with the good wishes of the student body, and we trust that his life's pathway might be as smooth as his native land is rough.

name: John Huland Carmical
description: Yep! He's our editor-in-chief, but the strangest thing about "Mike's" biography is that it is the only thing in the whole PANDORA which he reads for the first time in the finished volume. Inflicted with a chest of hot air, rivaled only by a Bunsen blow-pipe, he became possessed with the idea, early in his career, that he could speak. He has also fooled the judges to a certain extent, but there are always a few vacant seats in chapel when he rises to blaze forth in oratorical stimulation. If he continues to practice, in the militia district of Red Oak, the same system of politics that he has used here, we predict for him a forced and speedy return. Let us wish in his behalf that old "Comical" is back with us next year. He needs it, boys!

name: George B. Clonts
description: Graduate of Young Harris. Greet him as he greets you, with a smile (or laugh, if you can't help it). George is a very bright young man, adapting himself readily to civilization. Coming to us as he did from that illustrious institution, Young Harris, a school lost in the wild-cat haunts of North Georgia, he has learned with a very little practice how to play the victrola in the Y. M. C. A. This feat, however, does not mark the limit of his achievements, for before he had been here two years he had grown used to seeing a newspaper every day instead of once every two weeks. We owe him much credit, however, for amply filling the shoes of the only original "Co-Op." Garner, as master of Georgia's un-co-operative graft association. (We might add that a man with such feet is qualified to fill anybody's shoes.) George has been an elegant student and we regret that his stay with us has been only two years instead of four.

name: John Tennith Coffee, B.S.A.
description: Quiet, serene and gentle as a cat, reserved to the point where only a close friend can break through and reach him—that's his nature, boys, to a gnat's toe-nail. But Jack is an industrious little fellow. His ideal in life is to become the leading farmer in his county and unless we judge him wrong he will some day be a successful grower of cotton, peanuts, and South Georgia razor-backs. To some Jack's prevailing attitude is one of pessimism and gloomy outlook. "That's just the way of it, fellows," is his most characteristic expression, and with a sad, funeral-like attitude, he pours forth a doleful lamentation about the burden imposed upon a fellow by the unsympathetic world. But Jack merely sees life as it is, and no doubt he will not long brood over these conditions, for he is at heart a ladies' man and they are sure going to fall for him. Anyway, who can be a pessimist when he enjoys the companionship of a little Georgia queen?

name: Ben Philip Cohen, B.S.C.E.
description: No kin to Philip. He snoops around from home to Moore College, never heard of, never seen, not even is he known by Sam Kassewitz, Irwin Levy, nor Y. Lyons Joel. The only time he is known to come out of his hole is at elections, when he is dragged out Chapelwards by ambitious politicians. Yet, ye gods, what a shark! Yea, verily, he rivals Roger West in sharkism. Civil engineering is his long suit, and he weeps at the thought of the Panama Canal having been finished before he got out of college. Ben is a product of the town long ago made famous by Harold Diedrich Myer.

name: John Scott Coleman, B.S.
description: Here is a boy we do not understand, and yet we like him notwithstanding. For a couple of years he was just an ordinary sort of fellow who liked to play football, shoot pool, and go to dances, or do anything else that offered excitement. And then the great change. If he speaks to you on the street now it is a memorable occasion, but for that matter he is never on the street, except on his way to the postoffice. We thought perhaps that the change was due to some fair lady becoming neglectful of his charms, but in that case there would be no use in daily journeys for the mail. No, the mystery is too deep for us. Anyway, luck to you, John, and hopes for a speedy recovery.

name: Frank Columbus David, B.S.A.
description: Captain Frank C. David—the lad with the big mouth. He is captain of the great big company of little biddy runts. During his off hours he dispenses cigars at the Georgian Hotel and is mighty popular on Sundays when the Judge West dictum is in force. Frank is exposed to a course in agriculture. He ought to be a good farm hand when he gets his "dip.," for he is still a mighty big countryman. Frank couldn't have come from the heart of Columbus, but probably from the suburbs. He probably remembers that wild night ride he took a certain Sunday night in March.

name: Francis Florence Davidson, B.S.A.
description: Shady Dale-on-Central (reminds you of Stratford-on-Avon) is the city whence Ty hauls his guano. When one knows that this vicinity is also the habitat of Casey Jones, one wonders if it is a regular little "Lavy Jones' Locker." Once upon a time in the early annals of our glorious class, Ty wrote his name indelibly as a hero, by succumbing to the realms of unconsciousness in that famous pushball game. By making love to J. Mattie Hatcher in true Romeo style, Ty annexed himself to the ancient order of the Thalians.

name: Eugene Paul Drexel, B.S.A.
high school: Second District Agricultural and Mechanical School
description: "Tention men! Captain "Drex!" No braver soldier ever beat a retreat at the call of the bugle than Captain Drexel. His men hold him in such terror that they obey at least two commands out of every three commands given by him, and to use the poetic diction, "That ain't all." When the sophs. sheared his hair four years ago, he gave the Chancellor a few valuable dots on "How to run the university." He has been a steady, conscientious student, ever holding in view his worthy and noble intention of becoming a collector of Octagon soap wrappers, and to be as a side issue, a county demonstrator of agriculture. If "Drex" continues to grow as he has during his four years' stay here, he will soon be plowing four mules instead of one. Here's hoping!

name: Thomas Eugene Dunn
description: When he was young he was a leader in everything, a regular little ladies' man, a crack athlete, excellent bridge player, expert marble shooter, and sure top spinner, but lo! how is he fallen from his high estate, for alas, nobody has given him a chance. And all he boasts of is tennis champion, Glee Club man, and chief "Belliaker" of the university. You would hardly call him a mechanical genius, for he cannot put the padding under a hat band. This is parallel in bone-headedness to Seagraves reporting the U. S. Brewers' Association year book as a text-book of sociology. Tommie is a Cuthbertian.

name: Roland Ellis
description: When he was a Freshman they named him "Soapy," each letter of which has a meaning all its own. Since that time, just to get even with everybody his sole and only ambition in life is to get a good joke on someone and then proceed to see if he can run him crazy with it. Owing to the fact that he has indulged in this pastime for some four years, now he has become quite proficient and we think really deserves the title of champion goat-grabber. We certainly recall one occasion on which he got ours. When not thus dissipating, however, Roland is busy studying. His only amusements are a picture show infrequently and a drink at Costa's. He is very regular and punctual in everything he does, but there's one thing that he absolutely never misses and that is Sunday night supper.

name: Beverley D. Evans, A.B.
description: This is a curious combination. Beverley means well we're sure, but if he would just tell us so now, it would help a whole lot. The boy is slow of thought and slower yet of speech. Perhaps, it's all due to his wearing his head on the side of his face. The other side, being too much exposed, cannot function freely. "B. D." helps run his fraternity and gets along all right in classes, though, so maybe he will be a great lawyer some day. He and Cobb Dulaney ought to go into partnership. They could get their heads together on any subject with the greatest ease provided one of them looked backwards while the other looked forward. Could you beat it?

name: Alexander Robert Fawcett, A.B.
description: Captain Alexander Robert Fawcett is the full cognomen of our esteemed friend from Savannah. And proud is he of it. When he hit the classic city some four years since his breast was filled with ambition. The Rhodes' scholarship loomed up as his aim, but hard struggles on the field of Xenophon and the platform of Cicero painfully persuaded our young hero that he was no George Whitehead. Alec is as proud of his captaincy as McCall is of his majorship, or as Roosevelt of his colonelcy. His little idiosyncracies of speech do not keep back his generosity, nor do his defeats stifle his deeply rippling laugh and when Alec's face is clouded with frowns of wrath then shall the heavens fall.

name: Dozier Nelson Fields
description: Amid a covey of powder puffs, music rolls, curling irons, and other things of an unprintable nature he alighted at the university four years ago. He soon featured himself by the fact that he tried to propel himself instead of walking, and on one occasion was heard to say, 'Oh, bosh! This drilling is horrible.' However, he made a hit in the military department and was assigned two paces in the rear rank instead of one and was made to keep step in both. Dozier has demonstrated the fact that the day of miracles has not passed, and can now wield a safety razor equally as well as his roommate. In all class-room work the box score has always shown Dozier to be near the top, and as he leaves the university we can truthfully say that he has been a good student known and liked by all.

name: Lawrence James Fox
description: Lawrence is known for his athletic ability and on the gridiron, diamond and basketball court few have shown more skill, and certainly none more pluck than he. 'Cutie's' achievements, however, have not been limited to athletics. Since coming to college he has been unanimously picked as all-Southern Sophomore, has learned to use the safety razor, and has been an active member of the 'Co-Op.' Library, Y. M. C. A., Glee Club, and pressing club. Last year he was featured on the front page of the Atlanta papers in a six-reel Lochinvar act (only he wasn't the Lochinvar). Having recovered from the shock, however, he has recently been engaged as an artist's model at Lucy Cobb Institute. Fox's good nature has won for him more friends than he knows what to do with, yet he continues to make them.

name: Archibald Gann
description: Archie has only been with us three years, but during that time he has done a little of everything except overwork himself. He would have done that only it was not necessary, and besides there wasn't any excitement in it. He is like Powell in never worrying, but always getting by. Archie has certainly done his duty towards keeping up the university's reputation for sporting blood. He is always among those present at the dances and at one time had quite a reputation as a lady-killer. It is rumored however, that he found it somewhat difficult to get along with all parties and so had to retire. Verily where singleness is bliss 'tis folly to have a mother-in-law.

name: Charles Gordon Garner
description: Known to his many victims as 'Co-Op.' Garner, this former financial wizard of Georgia's Co-Operative Graft Association has ruled supreme for the past year, as the master photographer of the Agricultural Hall. Although he is as honest as a judge of the supreme court, he has, however, no scruples about taking anything that he can get into his camera. It would be hard to tell by looking at 'Co-Op.' that he is a man of adventure, even though you might know that he comes from Granite Hill, Ga. One of his most startling adventures occurred in his Sophomore year. He received a free hair-cut for trying to be a Freshman two years in succession. He has, however, settled down to a somewhat steadier pace recently and from reports is, in his capacity of adviser to Dr. Soule, saving the agricultural department more money each year than the doctor's salary amounts to. Garner is a good track man and the school owes him much for his consistent work.

name: Thomas Bolling Gay, Jr.
clubs: R. M. Guess and L. A. Paine's organization
description: Bolling got a bad start, by rooming with Stewart Harris for one year. How it all happened we cannot divine. Be that as it may, Bolling found, after calculating the year's expenditures, he had purchased one more bar of soap than his scheming roommate. Next year he tried it with Noble Bassett, whom he found harder to manage than Stewart. He waded through elective Greek one year, then suddenly became engrossed in scientific pursuits, and hopped two courses under "Proty" in last year. He is a prominent member of R. M. Guess and L. A. Paine's organization, and yet he is a pretty good boy.

name: Henry Thomas Gillespie
description: We planted a dictaphone in H. T.'s (Hold Tongue's) room in order to find out if he ever talked to himself, but not a vibration came from the delicate little instrument. A periscope, placed with its mouth level with his window, failed to reveal any actions other than those used by the average human animal when it studies. His maiden appellation is Henry, but we call him Thomas Gillespie for short. In the spring time in his fancy, Henry's thoughts have turned—oh, well, to make it brief, Henry has distinguished himself by being the first of the seventeeners to join the ranks of the Benedicts. On the level "Gil" is as peaceful and easy going as a meadow brooklet and everywhere you will find him as welcome as the flowers in May-sville.

name: Abraham Samuel Goldstein
description: In discussing this curious biped one is convinced of the fact that "Abe" has inherited some strange and uncanny ability to prophesy uncertain events long, very long before they happen. On only two occasions has he been known to fail: one being the Georgia-Tech football game of 1916, and the other being the presidential election of the same year. For further particulars consult Dunn and Bradstreet for November. As a student Abe has been an especially bright light, except in Greek. His failure in this department may probably be explained by the fact that only English, French, banking and drawing can be learned in the pool room. Abe is a great student of theoretical and applied economics (mostly the latter) and deserves much credit for taking a degree in three years.

name: Orville Duane Hall
description: Our tributes will not glow for some reason, but if they would, we would write a glowing tribute to O. Duane Hall. This man for no less than four autumns has worn the scrub uniform through every scrimmage with the 'varsity on Sanford Field. Neither was this from any lack of pluck or stick-to-it-iveness, for certainly no one has shown more of these elements than he, but because there were always heavier, larger men than he. On the last game of the season, however, he was sent in and now he is a well-deserved wearer of the "G." In the classroom, too, "O. D." has been a faithful, steady worker and a believer in the things practical and worth while. During his Senior year, Hall has become a great lover of the "chickens," and he and George Saye now have a book at press on "How to make an incubator hatch 20 eggs out of 200." Duane's frank, sincere, jolly nature causes his many friends to regret that their four years' of companionship with him are almost at an end.

name: Andrew Stewart Harris
description: A person of renown, for lo! he is Joel Chandler Harris' grandson. His unofficial cognomen is "B. B." Some say it means "Big Boss." But we all know better. He is a mighty good tennis player; the fact is, he and T. Dunn are the best in college. And not only that, for by some hook or crook he manages to inveigle around 90 per cent. from all his professors. Stewart is one of those Atlanta products, and is a mighty sassy little devil.

name: Roy V. Harris
description: We have often wondered why Goldberg never engaged "Runt" as a model for his drawings. The only reason that we can find is that even newspapers have a limit to the things which they publish. Ever since his entrance here "Runt" has contrived to make politics a matter of uncertainty. In his early years he dominated the campus section of the "Mugwump" party until any further domination was impossible. The party died. Like "Teddy," however, any old party will do for him; in fact his high value in the estimation of his friends has been due solely to his unselfish nature and a desire to always do that which is right. Some day he will make the people of Wrens, Ga. (wherever that is), feel proud that they "brung him up."

name: M. Thurman Harrison
description: A long, lean, lanky, quiet kind of a clod hopper who has a rhythmic figure like a lamp post and a persistent inclination to hang out at the Q room. As a student he is especially fond of Billie Hooper's Latin in preference to the gentler studies (?). He claims that horse riding is not a good kind of sport anyway. At first he was one of the biggest Freshmen Candler Hall ever possessed, but after a short time he decided that he had sufficiently worn away his countrylike appearance, and in a frenzy he migrated, the same old freshie and at the end of his course we can say that he is still the same and will ever remain so. We bid him a favorable wind for his journey and we trust that the sailing will be good for him when he has to paddle the canoe for more than two.

name: William Doughty Hillis
enrolltime: entered on his career here one full year ahead of most of us
description: “Bill” entered on his career here one full year ahead of most of us and after taking A.B., for two years decided that he could do better in the high art of growing peanuts in his native South Georgia county and changed his course to agriculture. His home is at Girad and it is said that he and his brother are the only natives of the aforesaid city who have enjoyed the privilege of being students at the university. The local reports also state that “Bill” is a whiz in his own home town, especially among the ladies and that he has toyed with many a heart. However, so far he has played the game safely, but we cannot tell how long he will pursue his career free and unhampered, for smouldering in his breast, the careful observer will detect a wonderful capacity for domicile happiness. Here is hoping that after all the trials of university life you may succeed, Doughty, and “live happy ever after.”

name: Roy Dewitt Hutson
enrolltime: having spent a previous year at Tech
clubs: Vice-President of Y. M. C. A.
description: Roy came here greatly handicapped from having spent a previous year at Tech. After a warm reception by the Sophs., he proceeded to give the chancellor a few valuable pointers on the ethics of running the university. Soon after this with ungloved hands he strolled into the mysterious circles of the Y. M. C. A. where the fates have favored him in the circle of their "no politics." Roy has been a good, consistent student in the economics department, and it is a well-known fact that he can buy more bananas for a nickel than anyone else. Although not like "Dean White," such a lover of the French, his dislike for the German policies thrust him into military service where he has co-operated with Capt. Fawcett in teaching his company a love for that poetic command, "Ho." A climax to his career, however, was reached when "Proty" and Albert added him to their staff. But in spite of all these, he is a companionable chap, yes, a likable chap, and leaves with the good wishes of all his fellow classmates.

name: Howell Arthur Ingram
enrolltime: residence with us has been only three years
description: Look at his innocent countenance and stop and think. Did you ever see such a portrayal of innocence in anyone's visage? But allow us to tip you off to the fact that he, although his residence with us has been only three years, has been the most popular barber among the freshies. The university surely owes him a debt of honor, for had it not been for his influence, the development of the gentle art of severing the curly locks of "Mama's boy" from their throne of all-wiseness, would have suffered a great setback. He has an appreciation, far beyond the average for all other kinds of art. He has been a great lover of poetry and painting and has contributed a large number of beautiful verses to the collection of campus poems. His artistic inclinations, however, have not interfered with his studies for he has won a good record in the classroom.

name: Y. Lyons Joel, B.S.E.
description: Anyone that has had the shocking experience of viewing the bushy evidence of manhood cultivated upon Mr. Joel's upper lip, will readily understand why he is frequently dubbed "Wild Lions" Joel. Understand, we do not wish to insinuate that this moustache is unbecoming, but intend rather to convey the idea that it is becoming a nuisance to healthy eyes. As a model for next season's fashions Joel is the original "Kuppenheimer Kid." He is an orator of remarkable ability, but we regret to say that the time spent by him in Atlanta, New York, Bogart, etc. (note the climax) has deprived Georgia of a most renowned speaker. In an interview with a correspondent of the PANDORA, Mr. Joel is quoted as follows: "Yes, I can easily locate the Academic Building, for there is situated the office of the dean." At any rate, old top, you deserve the sheepskin, because you have run the race in two years.

name: William Clyde Jones, B.S.A.
nicknames: The man with the smile that won't wear off
description: The "Eternal Casey," or "The man with the smile that won't wear off," that's him. Handicapped as he was coming here, by being from the same militia district as "Tye" Davidson, he has now largely outgrown this, and aside from rooming with "Dave," no one would ever suspect that these two intricate characters haul their grinding to the same mill. "W. C." tried to keep a watch dog in his room during the fall to ward off stragglers and burglars, but he wouldn't eat the stuff they fed at the "Beanery," and began to take on such a lean and hungry look that Jones thought it best to put the pining canine in his suitcase when he started home for the Christmas holidays. During his four years' stay here "Casey" has fooled the faculty into believing that he knows more about farming than to plant "Guinea watermelons" in his cotton middles, and that "Board of censors" is going to let him get away with it. "Bill" has found a tender, prominent spot in the memory of all his classmates and associates. How do we know? Ask anyone who knows him.

name: Alonzo Morris Kelly
description: "Ah," saith Horatio, "we shall not see his like again." "Judge," for, of course, he couldn't be called anything else, hath a gravity of demeanor and loftiness of carriage which distinguishes him at a glance from ordinary mortals. It is said his only relaxation is an occasional smile at meal times over some witticism at Brother Ellis' expense. But that would make a horse laugh. Judge is also a captain and says he intends to offer his company's services in the event of war. We wish we had a company we could send, instead of getting shot ourselves.

name: George Guy Kemp
description: It is strange how some people take a strong and curious liking for some subjects here at the university. Guy's affections have always leaned toward botany, a subject in which he has for three years been taking the first course. Besides a botany student Guy is "some" athlete. In his Freshman year he was a star in the push-ball game and in his Sophomore year developed much track ability as an artist of speed when the Freshmen took their vice-president from his guardianship in the woods near Whitehall, Ga. He was sergeant of the battery during his Junior year and was expecting a captaincy this year, but his plans were thwarted by the abolition of his department of the military organization. While Guy has not been a brilliant scholar he has the all-round level headedness which will insure for him a successful future.

name: Hoyle Newton Kemp
description: Whenever you want to find Hoyle, the surest place to find him is at the postoffice, for he stays there a large part of the time impatiently awaiting a letter from his girl, who lives in the vicinity of Austell. We say vicinity because in fact she lives so far back in the woods around Austell that Hoyle has to knock the owls off the gate and holler "Hello" at the doorsteps when he goes to see her. Though much time is consumed in answering "pink-scented" letters, Hoyle has had time to play baseball and he has been a staunch and valuable member of the squad for the past four years. Being a good student in all departments of both academic and agricultural work, we see no reason why he should in the future fear the "high cost of living."

name: James Blanton Lenhardt
description: You would scarcely suspect it, but this young lad hails from Carnesville. It is said that when his father decided to send him to college, he had to twist his nose to get him on the train. "Len" got here in due time and took refuge in the Y. M. C. A. He has, therefore, lived a quiet life during his four years stay with us. Lenhardt has had much to worry him—rooming with Dan Magill and Jim Skelton, besides taking a course in Physics 4. He suffered in silence, however. We have noticed that he comes back from Mr. Jerre Pound's Normal School every Sunday looking very much at peace with the world.

name: Henry Lazaran Levington
description: Henry is an admirable product of the grand old State of Chatham, and is now in attendance at the Medical College in Augusta. While here he made numerous friends and we accredit this to his early alignment with Tom Murphy in politics. Henry used to room in Candler Hall, but decided later in his first year to move out in town, where free hair-cuts are not so generously administered. (He got his hair cut three times in three days.) Now that he is studying medicine and spending his dinner hour in the dissecting room, we predict for Henry a very varied career as a veterinary surgeon.

name: Irvine Morris Levy
description: Ladies and gentlemen, we take great pleasure in introducing to you, Mr. I. M. Levy, the greatest hot-air artist that ever distinguished the chapel stage at the University of Georgia. His slogan is "Be Ready," and you may take our word that Levy always lives up to it. No matter what topic comes up for discussion, he can always find the opposite side, and is ready to stand by it with a healthy blast. He even had the nerve to argue to the commerce department that he knew economics. This dodge failed to work, however, in the math. department. So long as he can get some unsuspecting "nut" to take a stroll with him and submit to his steady flow, just so long does Levy's soul bask in the sunshine of happiness. That he has done much work "for the good of the institution" is evidenced by the enviable record above.

name: Hinton F. Longino
clubs: Editor-in-Chief of the Red and Black
description: Lean, long-legged, lanky lieutenant Longino; of our two cheer leaders, he's one of the best. In fact, as a cheer leader he's a pretty good chorus girl. He is so slim that his roommate says he uses a fountain pen for a bath tub. He's all right 'till you mention Jake's French "Verba" Book, then—"xy&**!!dz." Hinton is now Imperial Potentate of the Red and Black, thanks to the G. O. P.'s and much handshaking. He can't see why being cheer leader isn't as great an honor as being chancellor. Besides attending classes Hinton's chief source of information is obtained by constant conferences with the redoubtable Dean Dudley. "Dean" Longino has rare conversational abilities, is a good mimicker, can tell a joke with good grace, and you mentally characterize him as being a "darn" good fellow.

name: James Allen Lowrey, Jr.
description: Although "Jim" has been registered at the university for four years, his chief efforts have been expended on a correspondence course with Brenau and Shorter. The coolness and logic of Jim's mind at a time when a conflagration took place in old College, was shown by throwing his typewriter out the window and letting his "Shorter Pillow" down with a rope. During his stay at the university he has always upheld what he thought was right, and if Tom Watson said it he proclaimed it with all the ardor that his oratorical voice and forceful gestures could give it. As a conscientious student, moralist, philosopher, mathematician, as well as a politician, Jim has always been on the job, and he has that rare quality which few men possess and that is that his word can always be relied upon.

name: Thomas Watson Lufburrow
description: In "Luf" we have a true collegebred man. In his exhaustive search for knowledge he has invaded every prep school in the State (including Mercer), and has extinguished himself from most of the colleges. His affinity for Georgia may be explained by his unquenchable thirst for Sophomore math. and a workable "boot-lick" in the commerce department. That which "Luf" does not know about commerce is not worth knowing, and some have whispered that the same might be said of what he does know about it. In his favor, however, it may be said that "Luf" has a full and ever ready "line" which he never fails to make use of in a difficult situation, and which has won for him a healthy flock of friends among the students and faculty.

name: Marvin Pink Moore
clubs: Business Manager of the Red and Black
description: The Germans with all their scientific genius have never achieved more efficiency than has Pinkey. He is a genius so far as boning goes and he has certainly done his share of it. His chief mark of distinction is that he has quietly and in an unassuming manner toiled along the road of success, often accomplishing feats over which others have stumbled. His efficiency and never tiring efforts have caused the Red and Black editors to fear lest his "ad." department take from them sufficient space for their articles. Whatever Pinkey has accomplished he has worked hard for, and certainly deserves credit for his accomplishments. And whatever job he picks out to wrestle with in the future, we feel certain that Pinkey will come out on top.

name: John Ellis Mundy
clubs: Athletic Editor of the Red and Black
description: How it looks and how it talks! But beneath its rough and rugged surface lies an everlasting flow of wit, not equaled even by the well renowned Goldberg. It has a genius for poetic witticism and it has never failed to display its bounteous supply whenever opportunity offered itself. Yet it possesses not only a capacity for witty sayings, but has a peculiar inclination to the roaming instinct which shows itself occasionally when a certain Normalite spends the week-end with relatives in town. It is only through this instinct that his constant attendance at the Baptist Sunday School during his Senior year can be explained. It should make a splendid exhibit in the nut collection at its home county fair, for such specimens are rare. Therein lies our pride in claiming it as a Georgian.

name: Howard Henry McCall, Jr.
description: There's about as much difference of opinion about Howard around the campus as there is about Hoke Smith over the State. At any rate he has only been here three years and is into nearly everything in college—one of those 'dad-blamed' little cusses who try to take all the honors at Georgia. You never saw such a greedy little devil. There are many here who believe that Howard thinks too much of H. H. McCall, Jr., and possibly they're right. We will say this for him, though: that whatever he does he does with all his heart and does it well, be it Thalians, basketball or studies.

name: Augustus Cecil McPhail
description: Cecil came into our midst with a great future behind him. He made a great "rep" in the Atlanta Prep. Schools contest by defeating John Stewart with his speech on John E. Cook. His chief occupation here, though, is managing the Thalians O, would that David Belasco may now lay eyes on him, for then would our Cecil wear the buskins no longer in our midst. There are many things that he is famous for. Among the more well known and conspicuously noticed are an idiotic laugh and Camel cigarettes. 'Tis whispered about that his Big Ben wakes him all during his otherwise peaceful slumber (from 2 to 8 A. M.) to whiff the aromatic odor of his beloved Camels. O, Cecil!

name: Leon Alexander Paine
clubs: President of Y. M. C. A.
earned a "G" as a member of the squad
description: Our next victim on the slaughter block is L. A. Paine, of Valdosta, Ga., president of the university Y. M. C. A. His experiences as an "attender" of classes in commerce and economics has been of great aid to him in his Y. M. C. A. work. We call him "Tad" and we do well to call one "Tad" whose initials are "L. A." Though of little service to the PANDORA this year, Tad's picture as president of the Junior class formed a valuable supplement to the comic section of the last issue of this publication. He is a plucky little fellow on the gridiron, and has earned a "G" as a member of the squad.

name: William Louis Patman
clubs: Member of Glee and Mandolin Club
description: Louis Patman, the boy billiard wonder and also poolist, pre-eminent. Yes, he goes to class now and then, but we are speaking now, owing to limited space, of his chief occupation. Frank Postero says Louis is a boy after his own heart, which means that Pat has already purchased two tables with a full set of balls and cues and is well on the way towards a third. However, he used to give Lem McKenzie a tight game and he says he knows he can beat McClellan shooting seven and a bank. Pat seems especially fond of sciences in the university, making life miserable for Dr. White and "Proty." For which reason, if no other, he should have a space in the hall of fame as a benefactor of humanity and a champion against its oppressors.

name: Russell Rano Petree
description: WINGATE and PETREE. This "heavenly" or "hellish" pair (whichever you prefer) are called so because neither is uglier nor better looking than the other, which, gentle reader, if only you will refer to the other picture you will confirm me in my opinion. They are neither of the same age nor do they live on the same R. F. D., but since coming to college each has found himself indispensable without the other. Wearing the same kind and color of clothes, after having used the same brand of washing powder, one often sees them with derbies perched aloof on their gentle craniums and swinging their canes in mid-air, strolling serenely about the campus. They have made a big hit among the fairer sex, but whether it has been due to beauty or brass we decline to say. They have acquired great proficiency in the art of Terpsichore (continued under Wingate)

name: Julian Killen Quattlebaum
description: "Julie" is one of the most conscientious boys in the school, although he does not belong to the Y. M. C. A., or take a very active part in the "uplift" work of the university. "Julie's" main form of recreation is getting down on the baseball field every day of practice about two hours before everyone else with about four little negro boys, and bat to them. This earnestness on his part will no doubt this year earn for him his letter in baseball. Everyone has their faults, however, and "Julie's" is his persistent cursing, often indulging in profanity such as "good gracious alive," and when vexed or irritated he will go farther and say, "my goodness gracious alive." Aside from this we can find no fault and we earnestly hope he will overcome this, and make Bullock county one of its most respected citizens.

name: Marion McKenzie Reid
description: We had rather somebody else wrote this, because there's a limit to the powers of expression and then there's a censor, too. Isn't he a sweet sample of young America, though! We know some girls aren't overburdened with sense, but how he gets by even with a vacuum is beyond us. He gives all the girls a tremendous rush at dances and on Sunday nights, and is also rather fond of riding around in their cars, preferably in the back seat where he can be thoroughly comfortable. But did you ever hear of him taking a girl anywhere that it cost a dime? No, you didn't, and the girls wouldn't advise you to hold your breath until he does. He and Jim Ray are certainly true brothers in every sense of the word. They ought to form a partnership and sell ladies' ready-to-wear.

name: Walter McDowell Rogers, A.B.
description: The merry jingle of numerous high school mementoes first attracted the attention of the innocent bystander to Mac when, four years ago, he stepped on the campus eager for new forensic achievements. To hear his debate record in his high school days is a never-to-be-forgotten hot-air feast. With varying success he has entered here the arena of society, politics, oratory, military and the Y. M. C. A. Anyone who has had the opportunity of seeing him, a Senior with his derby and cane, perambulating down the campus walk, cannot help but realize that some Seniors should wear red caps. Good luck to you, Mac. We've all got a glad hand for you whenever we meet again.

name: George Paul Saye, B.S.A.
enrolltime: at the beginning of our race
description: Although he may now seem very gentle, the university has had a difficult task in domesticating this strange animal of unknown species. Born and reared in sight of a North Georgia mountain, where the gentle breezes waft fresh from the frosty top peaks, he joined us at the beginning of our race for the much-coveted "dip," and by four years of energetic plodding has at last been completely domesticated. Immediately upon his arrival here he became the favorite of the "ag." department's most famous "bull artists," on account of his ability to throw back the same grade of stuff with which he was fed. To prove that studies need not necessarily interfere with one's education, George has turned out to be a most ardent lover and an all-round ladies' man. Our best wishes go with Paul, and may he be the same old jolly fellow when in after years he sits by the fire-side bobbling a young mountaineer upon his knees, while his better half wafts the strains of a sweet melody, intermixed with sweet aromas from the adjoining kitchen.

name: James Robert Sealy, B.S.A.
description: Bob hails from the namesake town of the inventor of the phonograph. If you don't know where Edison, Ga. is, you will have to ask Bob, for we will be blamed if we know. Bob tried for two years and a half to make the Georgia baseball team and he got mad with the coach because he couldn't, and quit going out. Since that time he has been putting most of his time on his "ag." Also he's quite a ladies' man. He and Brother Frank David are just lady-killers! Bob is a good fellow, alright, and even if he does not win a maid of Athens, he will settle down with some country Jane and live a quiet, prosperous life out in the sticks.

name: Carl Calvin Seagraves, B.S.Ed.
enrolltime: entered the university as a Freshman
clubs: President of the Green Pinch-Back Cap Club
description: Mr. Seagraves, we understand, is from somewhere near Athens. Be that as it may he is here now and we have got to put up with him. Seagraves entered the university as a Freshman with our class and went along fairly well until he struck Latin. But one year at Young Harris enabled him to get off that condition and then he turned his attention to the more difficult courses in education. When he returned to college his Junior year he registered for the poetry 8 and 9 course under Prof. Roosevelt P. Walker. Seagraves told Prof. Walker that his name was C. C. Seagraves. Prof. Walker, with a puzzled look, could not tell where the C's left off and the name began, so he kept Carl after school and made him write his name 47 times. "C square" made himself famous when he bought his green pinch-back cap. He was instrumental in the organization of the Green Pinch-Back Cap Club, of which he was elected president. The other "Charlie" members of the club were T. N. Bassey, Doc Frierson, Jarrell Battle, W. C. Gheesling, and Heywood Hopkins. Seagraves is a hard worker and attends to his own business. He has never cut a class during his entire stay at the university.

name: Roff Sims, Jr.
description: If you want a picture of virtuous industry, gaze on his countenance. Roff may have a vice tucked away somewhere, but it must be in his hope chest. At any rate we've been unable to find it. Some say that Roff's brain works like a Packard Twin six, but on the whole is something on the order of a Dodge. We have to admit that we like Roff, though, and admire him for a lot of things. If he wouldn't be quite so sanctimonious, and get a little pep, he'd be an all-round, good boy.

name: George Hiley Slappey
description: This day! A special one-"real" farce comedy, featuring "Highgate" Slapper, in "The Man from Marshallville"-a scream from start to finish. BRIEF SYNOPSIS OF PLAY Scene 1. Time: Freshman. Enter "Highgate" disguised as a cabbage. Scene 2. Sophomore year. Here impersonates a doctor at a football game staged between New College and Chandler Hall. Scene 3. Junior year. Portraying "Highgate's" unhappy wedded life with "old lady Baldwin"; divorce suit pending. Melson named as co-respondent. Scene 4. Senior year. Hero drowns his sorrow in hard, studious work. Exit George, with our friendship and best wishes.

name: Pinckney Welch Snelling
description: We can't say much about Pink for he is a son of the dean. He was becoming well-known until he bought some spectacles like Lee Bradbury and Y. Lyons Joel. Then he had to be introduced all over again. If he put as much time on chemistry and physics as he does on the Harvard catalogue, he'd probably make more than "C plus." He's one of the few boys in college who have sense enough to take Greek. Yet every other day he wonders why he didn't hop B.S.E., because at times Greek 4 is like what Sherman called war. Pink is a steady little fellow and we predict for him a famous "pasteurship."

name: Blueford Custus Spurlock
description: Familiarly known to the fair damsels of the classic city as "Blue." He has a well-earned reputation among his fellow students as the proud possessor of a "nasty foot," or in more polite parlance a master of the Terpsichorean art. In the estimation of Spurlock, Blueford is a great man, not having a superior in any field. There are no heights in any clime to which his mighty physique, urged on by his expansive chest will forbid him to ascend. There must be some great attraction weekly in Atlanta for "Blue," or else he is affected by wanderlust, because he regularly forsakes the portals of knowledge, and either seeks consolation from a daughter of the Capital City, or else gratifies himself by views of the beautiful towns between the two historic cities. Blue is an enviable product of "B. H. S." of Atlanta, which he interpolates to mean "Blue Him Self."

name: John Pendleton Stewart
description: Old John, the boy who sticks by his guns. We believe in you even if you do wear old-fashioned spectacles. He manages to do about all there is to be done on the campus and still have time for a few dances and such. There is one thing about John that ought to take him a long way in this world, and that's his ability to look at all three sides of a dollar bill before letting it fly to parts unknown. If the Thetas could just have put him and Brother Harris both to act as treasurer they would be worth a million dollars in no time. Get old John Pingling Stuum cornered and you find him rather wittily inclined, even though he is a little timid and prone to blushing. Some say he is the best all-round man in the Senior class.

name: Dennis D. Still
description: The first time we laid eyes on this chap was in Bob Park's English class. We can understand why he took this—because he had to in order to get his degree. But he whirled in and took the drama course under Prof. Park his Senior year. Now what puzzles us is what can a man like Still want with drama. Probably, the fillers down at Peabody Hall ran out. This man's initials are D. D., but don't you fool yourself. He is not a doctor of divinity, by any means. Far be it from us to tell you just in what line he would best qualify, but we will drop a gentle hint. He and "Mac" Putney are running neck and neck for first place. Denny certainly has the right surname—not that he has been making moonshine or selling it, but he is about the stillest man we ever saw. He is a past master in the art of keeping his business to himself. With this quality, he should make the government a cracking good secret service man. We don't know what kind of work this youth has picked out, but whatever it is, if he is still the Still he still is he will come out of the fray with bells on.

name: William Parker Tabor, A.B.
description: Coming fresh from Madison county, where, as he says, the goldenrods and the buxom girls bloom all the year round, he entered the university with the most of us, having a scared look of anticipation, which forced him to take up his nightly abode in the old chapel walls, where he spent many a restless night dodging the official shearers. But even the worst of wild animals can be tamed and he has been no exception. However, it might be said that his tamers have encountered considerable difficulty in getting him accustomed to the ever busy street cars. He has also been such a loyal and devoted admirer of his home girls that even the bravest of boys have never been able to induce him to take the slightest peep into the local circles of society. He has been a consistent and ever plodding student and beneath his smooth and easy-going surface you will find plenty of character and good practical knowledge, which has won for him many friends who will ever hold him in their memory.

name: Edmund Brewer Tate, B.S.
clubs: Member of the Gridiron and Senate Clubs
description: Popularly known as "Rube," he has roamed on the campus for lo, these five long years, proving by his antics that his name is alas, too appropriate. When a Sophomore he used, on his hands around his room, for hours at a time, to turn all sorts of somersaults, handsprings, etc., practicing to be a football player. Next year he won the coveted place. Rube gets an unaccountable pleasure out of massacring poor innocent little frogs, cats, mosquitoes, stray dogs, etc., in the Zo. lab. Then comes home and delivers lectures by the hour on his discoveries, for his faculty for absorption is marvelous. Here of late, he has been mighty regular at donning his flashy bow tie and hiking it over to Brenau. Here's to you, Brewer, old top.

name: Thomas Atkinson Thrash
description: They messed up the PANDORA last year with his pedigree and Senior picture, so naturally we thought we had seen the last of him. But having roped in the football captaincy, he is back at us this year to inflict another nightmare upon the photographer and to fling his great achievements in the face of innocent readers. So here goes—Hiding behind the shield of the Phelps-Stokes (or Jokes) Scholarship, Cap'n Tom has, for the past year been making an extensive study of "High Browns." If Tom burns the midnight juice to the same extent that he has caused the editors to waste it in writing his biography, we must hand it to him for diligence. Tom is of massive stature, and had he lived in the classic Greek age, he would undoubtedly have been known as "The Earth Shaker." (For further references on this interesting specimen, see the last three issues of this publication.)

name: Daniel Hess Upshaw
description: Daniel Hess Upshaw is from Between, Ga., but judging from the above he is ashamed of it. He said this hamlet had a population of 49 when he left, but now alas! only 48. When he goes back to the old homestead it ain't gonta take him long fer to be a hayseed agin', 'cause he ain't never got fer away from being a countryman. Howbeit, Dan is a fiery debater, as Irvin Levy may well remember. His main object is to get his "dip.," then get married. Even now it is all Bob Park can do to hold him off till June. Well, Daniel, we wish you a most successful career.

name: Oscar David Watson, B.S.A.
clubs: assistant business manager of the Ag. Quarterly
description: Step right up, gentlemen. We want you to examine this picture and see how closely it resembles the physiognomy of a certain man who teaches physics, and who was once very much put out over the loss of his slide rule. The thing that has puzzled us is what did this youth and Hess Upshaw find in common? However, we think that M. W. H. Collins got some good work out of him when he was assistant business manager of the Ag. Quarterly. At any rate it took the business ability of Co-Op. Garner to get the durned thing back on the map when they got through with it. In society he and Putney are bitter rivals; however, we think the pink letters from Milledgeville will safely land O. D. Watson for the G. N. I. C. belle.

name: George Hall Westbrook, B.S.E.
clubs: President of the Junior Law Class
description: Commonly known among the boys as "Eastereek," Hall Westbrook makes his bow at the great pill-slinging right paw of the Georgia baseball squad. We no longer believe in miracles, but they are happening right along. It will be hard to make the natives of the metropolis known as Ila, Ga., believe that "Eastereek" is the same lad whom they saw blindfolded and strapped in the express car going toward Athens five years ago. His progress here has been rapid and brilliant, and has been due, no doubt, largely to his strict adherence to his motto: Make a hit. He has demonstrated his ability as an orator and debater in his capacity of president of Demosthenian, and frequently moved his audiences from tears to laughter, by his graceful figures of speech. East is a lover of poetry and politics, but it has been rumored that Hall's one ambition is to own a tobacco plantation and consume the entire output. Whate'er your plans be, "Easty," we wish you success!

name: William Osmond White, A.B.
clubs: Captain and Adjutant Corps of Cadets
description: When Osmond entered college he was much chagrined by the fact that he could not obtain a front seat in chapel as well as in all of his classes and was heard to remark in a tone of disappointment, "Ham, I Gorry." For four years he has tried to obtain notice and to curry favor with the faculty by assuming many different kinds of unnatural positions and attitudes in the classroom. His career has featured in the library in that he could yell "Closing up" louder and sooner than anyone who has ever graced the frigid and undusted region of the building commonly known as the library. This practice has also greatly aided him in his oratorical and Napoleonic ambition. "Dean," the appelation by which he is known by his many friends, leaves the university with a record which one has a just cause to envy, and when the call is made for men on the job it is agreed that he will be among those present.

name: Shelton Elliot Wilson, B.S.
description: A young, blushing fellow, with a maidenlike modesty, and a very pleasing countenance, called Wilson, blew up to Athens from the sweltery regions of Savannah, Ga. But he was doomed not long to remain in obscurity as he immediately became so attached to LeConte Hall that the distinguished title of "Proty" was also given him. He left us at the end of his second year to take up a four-year sojourn at the Medical College preliminary to becoming a prescription writer and a dealer in appendicitis cases. Somewhat a favorite of the fairer sex of the heart-breaking kind, he should make a hit in his old burg.

name: Harry Lynwood Wingate
description: (Continued from Petree) and at every performance of the "stenographers' ball" they have lent strong and willing hands and arms. Their gentle carcasses have never failed to greet the passing friend with a broad smile and a broader hand. Individually, each is a giant; collectively they are two giants. We all remember the adage: "United we stand, divided we fall," but in the case of Petree and Wingate, due to their unusually firm foundation or, in the words of the poets, their "good understanding," they will continue to stand and the winds of time will never dislodge them from their moorings. (Nautical term). By their well-known achievements on the gridiron and by their congenial natures they have won for themselves the esteem and good will of every student.

name: Theodore Markland Yates
description: Another wandering fellow who migrated to his home town to be confined in the clinics and have rooms at the Medical College. With a head of indescribable shape, containing ideas not yet expressed, he should be a fair specimen for the collection down at Augusta. Like the rest of our "Pre-meds," he is a rare bird of a peculiar plumage who very early assumed the owl-like appearance of the handler of drugs and the user of the knife. A good student he is and much more could be said in his favor had he not alighted on our campus in company with Skinner. Our best wishes go with him.

name: E. O. Hunter
clubs: Secretary and Treasurer, Senior Law Class

name: George Fagin Baldwin
description: Standing at the head of the class roll, as "Toby" does, the chances of escape from the deadly fire of the profs. are diminished a hundred fold. So many absences were registered by the doctor that George got a habit of answering "present" good and strong when entering the room, although long before class time. George has always had a habit of attending to his own business and attending to it well, so we don't see why he shouldn't handle other people's all right. And he is a good scout to boot, which always helps a lot. We are sorry the old gentleman will have to give him up so soon.

name: Tom Reid Beasley
description: Early in the history of this class Tom erred in allowing it to become known that he was on the football squad. From that time forth he has had a real hard time in convincing a certain member of the faculty that he had heard of that treatise on the common law by Mr. Justice Blackstone. This is the boy who had so much fun chasing chickens in the by-gone days of innocent youth. Tom is very regular in his habits, sending and receiving letters every day, which emit sweet odors upon opening, and upon reading cause big chunks of happiness to overcome the grouch received in the old man's class. He is a good athlete, but for every scratch received he sends his "would be" a telegram informing her of its serious nature.

name: James Morgan Bartow Bloodworth
description: You may not believe it, but all this long name was given James Morgan Bartow at such a tender age, that he was unable to object except by whining, and this youthful habit has stuck to him ever since. Morgan is a very busy man all the time. He assumes a business attitude, even when he is in the university gymnasium taking exercise with his other giant companions (Bob Wesley and Floyd). Outside of the chancellor and the dean, Bloodworth considers himself next in importance when a grave question confronts the university. Bloodworth is considered not only by his classmates, but also by the profs. as one of the best students in his department. His activities are not confined, however, to class work alone. He is also a politician and a close friend of the Y. M. C. A. of the first order. We drink a toast to "Bludwine's" success.

name: Benjamin Hugh Burgess
description: Hugh is a very close friend of Mr. Ball, but aside from that he is a pretty good fellow. When we first saw him, we thought he looked very much as if he had just come from Tech., but he has improved considerably since then. One thing we notice about Benjamin is that he always wears a pained expression between 9:15 and 10:10 a. m., and that he keeps his face turned towards the window as much as possible. Certainly this will show him to be of a disposition after our own heart. In fact we can appreciate his feelings fully.

name: Henry Maynard Carrere, Jr.
description: Gee, golly! The old gentleman does sit down hard on this little boy. We think the reason is that Carrie is the only man in the class smaller than the Reverend. Any way we admire his spunk. He gets right under the gun and takes everything that comes without sounding a retreat. We always have suspected that he wears an invisible gas mask during these early morning hours else how could he survive? In disposition and in looks he is quite chipper, especially when in attendance at the Manicure Club. When we first saw Henry Jr., we were in some doubt as to whether he was a Frenchman or a rooster or both. Since then we have concluded that it is "not all wrong," and only hope that his career won't be cut as short as Carrere.

name: Gilbert Xavier Cheves
description: Buck is quite a distinguished gentleman. A pet of the old man and deservedly so, the roommate of Squire Gillis, from which, of course, it is unnecessary to add that he is a politician of a high order, quite a clever performer on the baseball diamond and not at all bad on the "green." He is a fully paid up member of the Manicure Club and will soon begin to draw dividends. We don't know whether Buck intends to practice law or not, but whatever he does we believe he will make plenty of money and friends.

name: Robert Lee Cooper, LL.B.
description: Swe-e-t, sw-e-e-t Robert. Ah, what can we say more? Gentle reader, forebear if words fail us here. We are but human and now we come to what is not. Gladly would we solve this mystery and eagerly expound to the wondering world the whys and wherefores of his being. But, alas! our learning is but limited. We can but wonder and wondering, wonder more for we have never studied embryology. Let us leave it to the Smithsonian Institute. Perhaps there may have been a precedent in the stone age, but if there was we are confident this is the leading case.

name: Landon Cobb Dulaney, LL.B.
description: Cobb came a long way just for the pleasure of sitting in the amen corner of the old gentleman's morning gathering. We have heard, though, that there is another corner far removed from the dry and dusty atmosphere of Blackstone and law where Cobb may be seen any clear night (or rainy, either, for that matter) softly conversing with one of fairer countenance and softer tones than those of our beloved doctor. When exam. papers on pleading were returned it was discovered that Cobb had made 98. At first we wondered, but now we have cause to believe that he could make a 100 without the least effort. You see he has had extensive practice in that line, although yet without a license. We understand that he would like to buy one, though, at the earliest possible date. Well, luck to you, Cobb. We hope you're as certain of getting license No. 2 as you are of getting license No. 1.

name: Henry Mitchell Dunn
description: Ah! here's another poor lovelorn lad. This thing must be catching. The only trouble is the girls aren't too. Hank believes in General Grant's policies, though, so there may be hope after all. He seems to be leading the field anyway. Speaking of field, Hank wanders down on the baseball diamond now and then and when he is in the humor for it has been known to pitch no-hit games. And on the "green," too, he is very clever with his cue and sometimes with his putter. Hank sticks close to the amen corner in all his classes and seeks to subdue the enemy by the fierceness of his gaze, in which we must admit he seems fairly successful, having even been known to scare Sylvanus into giving him a ten.

name: Edward Lee Floyd
description: As might be gathered from the alphabetical section after his name, Brother Floyd is a distinguished gentleman. In fact, he is a landmark in these parts. We always have liked "E. L.," and we believe that he is going to make a good lawyer. If being a shark counts for anything he should. His great thirst for knowledge is not easily quenched, for not content with exposing himself to "Old Fort Sylvanus" for nine months during the year, he spends the summer months juggling the law with H. Abit by day, and by night he takes the "campus course" with the fairest of Georgia's "peaches." We wish we could report more extensively on Edward's doings in the university, but as most of them were before our day we must be satisfied with this.

name: Neil Lee Gillis, Jr.
enrolltime: since the memory of man runneth not to the contrary
description: Well, we'll have to admit he's pretty good at doing it. Neil has been in the university since the memory of man runneth not to the contrary. During that time he has accumulated great honors, mostly electoral, for as a politician he is in a league all by himself. Speaking of leagues, he is about as good at catching baseballs as he is votes, which may explain why he is 'varsity captain. You hear lots of talk about the expense of college education and the high cost of living, but we understand that Neil makes a comfortable profit on his education and then some. We don't blame him for that, though. Anybody that's been "Borey" of the G. O. P. (Grafters Only Permitted) as long as he has ought to be worth a small fortune. We believe he will be worth a large one some day if he doesn't stick too close to the farm. Neil's long on popularity and short on speed, but he'll get there just the same.

name: Wilder Quinton Gresham
description: Here the writer almost balks. We believe that W. Q. is some poker artist, because he runs so many bluffs in the classroom and usually wins out, and they say he has the reputation of being good at "holding hands." But those who are members of the "Manicure Club" cannot hold this against the mountaineer. In addition to his card-playing ability he is a shark and the proud possessor of a Phi Beta Kappa Key. Gresham, we firmly believe, would make a great lawyer, but he informs us that his intention is to go back to his mountain hut and there enjoy life "moonshining," and defending his accomplices who are not shrewd enough to dodge the "revenuers."

name: EDWARD ORMONDE HUNTER
description: We couldn't decide about Eddie for a long time. Cobb says he used to be scared of him at first, but we note he has gotten over it now and he has been on aforesaid Eddie's "hip," ever since, aided and abetted by Shuford, Theus & Co. But for them and the fact that the university hasn't a wrestling team we believe Eddie would be perfectly happy, for he has an unlimited supply of note book paper and his fountain pen is always in good working order. E. Ormonde's only dissipation is an occasional game of billiards. He can only shoot one game every day or so because he always looks up each leave in Corpus Juris and the Code before shooting. The rest of the twenty-four hours is devoted to studying and recitation with perhaps a little sleep and eats.

name: RICHARD NEWTON MATHIS
description: Mathis was captured near Fitzgerald and was brought to the university of Georgia nearly two years ago and placed in the law department. Law has been Mathis' master since entering here and no slave could have served his master more faithfully than has he. By unceasing study Mathis has obtained a very high mark in law, but is forced to wear braces to uphold his shoulders as a result. He has a mind and manner all to himself and up to date has never agreed on any issue with his classmates. "We admire his spunk, but damn his judgment." He has many friends and we predict for him a most successful future as a librarian in some one volume library. As a writer Mathis admits he is our best. When prizes for law essays were announced he informed Prof. Green that instead of "Cye." he wanted Corpus Juris. The contest is not over. Mathis has been one of our best students and has done much good work in the law department.

name: Warren R. Mixon
description: We like "Mix" very much when he mixes drinks, but not when he collects money for the Y. M. C. A. (Young Money Collectors' Agency), or accompanies Spillers in harmonious ditties at the Methodist church. Warren is a good swimmer, (he admits it himself), being a member of the "Baldhead row" in the old man's class, and has not cut a class since the memory of man runneth not to the contrary. He smokes almost as much as Weston, on the theory that it is better to smoke here than hereafter. The "R" in Mix's name is for distinction, so he says, but we all know Warren without that. Mixon is a hard worker in the law department and we believe that he will make a good lawyer, and we advise him after completion of his law course, to practice law and not preach, as his tendencies lead to preaching.

name: Joseph Louis Morrison
description: Morrison tried a year of academic and then dropped into the ranks of the lawyers, one of the favorite short courses to a degree. We must admit that we fail to see where he found time for so trifling a matter at all, so extensive and varied are his activities in other lines. He can play anything from basketball to butler with the exception of billiards, and as a politician he is somewhat of a "whang," although he did bet on Hughes. He and Brother Howard, between them, have run for everything in the university except chancellor, and it's a good thing there wasn't a vacancy there, because both would have insisted on being elected. Some people accuse Louis of being conceited, and we have thought so at times ourselves but as a matter of fact we've finally reached the conclusion that he is not, although he might have some excuse for it. J. Louis will make a good business man, politician, or actor, but not a lawyer.

name: Francis Otey McClellan
description: Francis Otey, how can we ever thank our neighbor State for your presence at the university? We can only say that if it ever happens again we shall declare that a state of war exists. Otey is the only man in college who has succeeded in making society inexpensive so far as he himself is concerned. He has a habit of announcing social festivities and then charging one dollar per head before allowing anyone to participate. His expression becomes saintly when in attendance at the Manicure Club, but when disappointment stares him in the face no Grecian herald ever made better time than he to proclaim the news. Otey is an all-around man. As good a Mug-Wump as they get to be; his only fault is that he never helps anyone to office but himself. He should make a great U. S. senator when a filibuster is needed, for he has never been known to agree with anyone. He doesn't believe minds of great men run in the same channel. His fleetness of foot, however, should be of great assistance to him in chasing clients on the banks of the Mississippi.

name: Howard Hart McWhorter
description: Billy Powell is accused of being about as lazy as you make 'em, but we must confess we think "Mac" deserves the title. He can't even get along with Tommy, so you know he doesn't let studying interfere with his education. Everybody likes Howard, but he doesn't care much about himself. Howard says he is going to farm for a living. If he does maybe he will profit by some of Silvy's discourses on Georgia agriculture, and keep the land from standing on edge and running off into the creek.

name: Charles Inman Owens
description: Gentle reader, be not alarmed at the above specie; just spare us a moment and we will try to show you the greatness of little things. "Tators," as he is generally known by his fellow barristers, thinks he is the favorite son of the beloved doctor, but he is sadly mistaken. He is great, not only in the class-room, but alike on the baseball field. The latter he gained by keeping gate for the Albany team. "Tators," as he strolls about our beautiful educational center, to the pleasure of all the girls, has a very dignified and prosperous look, as if this were the smallest town he owns in the South.

name: Virgil Swanson Parham
motto: "Nomen et Omen," meaning "In God we trust,"
description: Gentlemen, let us stand and sing "Nomen et Omen," meaning "In God we trust," which, after taking one look at Virgil Swanson, we deem quite appropriate. We would like to state on behalf of this classic youth from Odessadale that there is no truth in the report that he is to open up his office in the M. & W. after graduation. We have the M. & W.'s word for it. We predict Virgil will be an excellent divorce lawyer, since it takes two terms of court to settle such cases properly Look out, Daddy Semmes!

name: Charles Grover Payne
description: Sheriff Payne, who somewhat resembles Mark Twain, and looks the part perfectly well, once taught school, and put forth his best efforts trying to educate the "swamp rats" of South Georgia. Having obtained this preliminary experience, and being a distinguished educator himself he immediately became a favorite of the dean, who out of his compassion for his fellow-educator never "shoots" Grover more than twice a week, provided "Pop" is not called on oftener to recite. C. G.'s knowledge of law is not entirely theoretical, and he, in its application, uses his great store of old common "horse sense," which we believe will insure him a successful future.

name: Henry Benning Pease
